question: Which of the following is a feature of Java?
description: Java is object-oriented, platform-independent (via the JVM), and avoids direct pointers and multiple class inheritance.

question: Which of the following is the correct syntax to print in Java?
description: Standard output uses System.out.println(...).

question: Which component is responsible for executing Java bytecode?
description: The JVM executes .class bytecode. The compiler produces it; the JRE provides the runtime.

question: Which of the following best describes the JRE?
option_a: A package of tools for developing Java applications
option_b: The environment for running Java applications
option_c: A program that compiles Java source code into bytecode
option_d: The virtual machine that runs bytecode
description: The JRE (Java Runtime Environment) provides everything needed to run Java apps. The JDK is for development; the JVM is the engine inside the JRE.

option_b: To read an integer value from the user
option_c: To read a string from the user
option_d: To read a boolean value from the user
description: nextInt() reads an integer from console input.
Post-increment: y gets 10 (old value), then x becomes 11 → 11, 10.

question: Which of these is the correct way to declare a constant in Java?
description: The final keyword declares a constant. const and immutable are not valid Java modifiers here.
a < b is true; then a++ < b evaluates 5 < 10 (true) and makes a=6; the body runs a += 2 → 8.

question: Which of the following is NOT a benefit of cross-platform development?
option_c: Access to platform-specific features without limitations
description: Cross-platform tools trade some native feature access/performance for reuse, lower cost, and faster delivery.

question: Can the main method in Java have any name?
option_a: Yes — any method name works as an entry point
option_b: No — the entry point must be named exactly main
option_c: Yes — as long as it is public
description: The JVM looks specifically for public static void main(String[] args); the name main is required.

question: Was Java developed as a write-once, run-anywhere language?
option_a: Yes — bytecode runs on any platform with a JVM
option_b: No — Java must be recompiled for each platform
option_c: No — it only runs on Windows
option_d: Yes — but only for web applications
description: Java's 'Write Once, Run Anywhere' portability comes from compiling to JVM bytecode.

question: What type of language is Java?
description: Java is both: source is compiled to bytecode, which the JVM then interprets/JIT-compiles at runtime.

question: How can you create an infinite for loop in Java?
description: for(;;) omits all three parts, looping forever. (Option A eventually overflows and is not reliably infinite.)
3 < 4 is true and 4 > 2 is true, so it prints A.

description: The string literal is missing its closing quote, a syntax (compile-time) error.

option_a: Syntax/compile error: variable x might not have been initialized
option_d: No error; x is used correctly
description: Java forbids reading an uninitialized local variable; the compiler rejects it with 'variable x might not have been initialized'.

question: Is Java's platform independence due to the JVM?
option_a: Yes — the JVM runs the same bytecode on any platform
option_b: No — it is due to the operating system
option_c: No — it is due to the source code being interpreted directly
option_d: Yes — but only because of Gradle
description: The JVM abstracts the underlying OS/hardware, so the same bytecode runs everywhere.

question: Is the JRE alone enough to develop Java applications?
option_a: Yes — the JRE includes the compiler
option_b: No — development needs the JDK (which adds the compiler and tools)
option_c: Yes — the JRE includes all dev tools
option_d: No — you need only the JVM
description: The JRE only runs apps. To develop (compile) you need the JDK, which includes javac.

question: Are Java programs converted to bytecode that runs on the Java Virtual Machine (JVM)?
option_a: Yes — javac produces bytecode that the JVM executes
option_b: No — Java compiles directly to machine code
option_c: No — Java runs as an interpreted script with no compilation
description: javac compiles source to bytecode, which the JVM then executes.

question: Which of the following is a cross-platform mobile development framework?
description: Flutter is cross-platform; Swift/Objective-C are iOS-native and Kotlin is Android-native.
